user research · ux/ui design · figma

Pitt Plugged

teamVaishnavi Kusumba, Alyssa Jacoby, Janet Zhang
roleUX/Product Designer
skillsFigma, User Research, Prototyping
ambitionMake music-sharing feel human rather than performative.
the problem

Why does sharing a song with someone feel riskier than just talking to them?

the process

Through interviews with Pitt students, including a marketing major, a comp-sci major, and a music major, we kept hearing the same thing: people love seeing what their friends listen to, but hesitate to share their own taste first. We built empathy maps for each interview, then pulled everything into one affinity diagram across four buckets (support, UI/UX, accessibility, and features) to find the real patterns underneath what people told us.

That's where three directions came from: a music posting board, a fully-anonymous take-a-song-leave-a-song exchange, and a music roulette feature. We prototyped take-a-song-leave-a-song first, using a simple Google Form that only let you submit a song after listening to someone else's. It taught us something important: full anonymity removed the stress, but it also removed the credit, since some people wanted others to know a recommendation was theirs.

Early wireframes for the home page and concert bulletin board flow.

the solution

Pitt Plugged lets students post songs, either anonymously or with their name attached, comment on what others share, and vote songs up or down a campus-wide trending chart. Local bands can post their shows to a shared bulletin board, complete with an auto-generated event poster. Friends can follow each other to see what's actually playing in each other's feeds, not just what they post.

The final home page, with onboarding help built in after usability testing.

Posting to the bulletin board, where a custom event poster generates automatically.

Following friends to see what they're listening to.

Two rounds of heuristic evaluation caught two severity-3 accessibility issues: text blending into background colors, and no way for less-intuitive features to be understood without guessing. We fixed both by recoloring for contrast and adding a short help page to every major screen.

what i learned

The biggest lesson was that removing friction doesn't always mean removing the right friction. Full anonymity felt like the obvious fix for music-sharing stress, but it also quietly removed the recognition some people actually wanted. The best version of Pitt Plugged wasn't the most anonymous one, it was the one that let people choose for themselves. I also learned how much a heuristic evaluation can reshape a "finished" design, since some of our best decisions came after we thought we were done, not during the original research.
